title: Spring in Production — Boundaries, Proxies and Failure Modes

sections.heading: What the container does — and what it does not do
sections.content:
Spring wires object graphs and applies cross-cutting behavior through proxies. That makes dependency injection, transactions, security and caching convenient, but the annotation is not magic. A call from one method to another method on the same instance can bypass a proxy; transaction boundaries, propagation and rollback rules must match the business operation.

Keep controllers focused on transport, services on use-case orchestration, and repositories/adapters on persistence or external I/O. Test the policy in a focused unit test and the framework wiring in an integration test.

title: JPA in Production — Persistence Context and Query Cost

sections.heading: Managed entities are not DTOs
sections.content:
Inside a transaction, JPA tracks managed entities and can flush changed state automatically. That is useful for aggregate updates, but returning managed entities from an API couples serialization to lazy loading and can trigger unexpected queries. Use projections or dedicated response DTOs for read endpoints.

Measure SQL before changing fetch types. An N+1 issue is a query-shape problem: choose a fetch join, entity graph, batch fetch or projection for the particular use case, then verify cardinality and pagination.

title: SQL Performance — Evidence Before Indexes

sections.heading: Read the actual plan
sections.content:
A slow query needs representative parameters, data distribution and an actual execution plan. Compare estimated and actual rows, then inspect scans, joins, sorts and memory spills. A sequential scan is not automatically wrong; an index only helps when its selectivity and order match the access path.

Every index improves some reads while adding write, storage and maintenance cost. Change one cause at a time, test with production-like data and monitor after rollout because data distribution changes.

title: HTTP APIs — Contracts, Concurrency and Recovery

sections.heading: An API is a long-lived contract
sections.content:
Status codes describe the broad outcome; a stable problem format gives clients a machine-readable error code, safe detail and correlation ID. Do not expose stack traces, SQL or authorization internals.

For mutable resources, retries and concurrency belong in the contract: use deterministic ordering for pagination, validators such as ETag and If-Match for stale writes, and idempotency keys for operations that cannot safely be repeated.

title: Testing Strategy — Map Risks to Evidence

sections.heading: Coverage is not confidence
sections.content:
Choose the narrowest test that proves the risk. Unit tests protect business rules quickly; integration tests prove database mappings, migrations, serialization and security wiring; a small number of end-to-end tests protect critical journeys.

When a defect escapes, first create a regression test that fails against the old behavior. Then ask which assumption was missing: input boundaries, production data, integration behavior, authorization, time or deployment configuration. Track flakiness and feedback time alongside coverage.

title: Async Work — Deadlines, Cancellation and Ownership

sections.heading: A timed-out future may still be running
sections.content:
A CompletableFuture timeout only completes the future exceptionally; it does not necessarily cancel database work or a remote side effect. Define an end-to-end deadline, configure client timeouts, close resources deterministically and make interruption handling explicit.

For externally visible commands, cancellation cannot undo work already accepted by another system. Use idempotency keys, status lookup or compensation, and distinguish a caller abandoning a wait from safely stopping the underlying operation.

title: Kafka Events — Durable Contracts and Recovery

sections.heading: Events outlive deployments
sections.content:
Producers and consumers deploy independently, and retained events can be replayed long after a release. Prefer additive schema evolution, stable event identities and compatibility checks in CI. Do not silently change the meaning of an existing field.

Delivery is commonly at-least-once: duplicates, retries and partial failures are expected. Use stable event IDs, idempotent consumers and an outbox when a database change and publication must remain reliable together.

title: Containers — Reproducibility, Security and Diagnosis

sections.heading: An image is a delivery artifact
sections.content:
Build once, identify the image by digest and promote that same artifact through environments. Multi-stage builds, a small runtime image, a non-root user, locked dependencies and a pinned base digest improve the supply-chain boundary.

When a container exits, inspect the exit code, logs, effective command, mounts, environment and resource limits before changing the image. A container is healthy only when its main process and its declared readiness behavior match the deployment contract.

title: Security — Threat Models and Authorization Boundaries

sections.heading: Authentication is not authorization
sections.content:
Authentication establishes who made a request; authorization decides which action that identity may perform on which resource. Enforce both coarse role rules and object-level ownership where required. Validate input, apply least privilege, protect secrets and ensure logs never contain credentials or sensitive personal data.

Start from threats: enumeration, privilege escalation, replay, injection and data exposure. A secure API has explicit failure behavior and tests for denied access, not only successful login.

title: Kubernetes — Declared State and Application Readiness

sections.heading: A Pod is not an availability guarantee
sections.content:
Kubernetes replaces failed containers, but the application must expose honest startup, readiness and liveness signals. Readiness controls traffic; liveness is for genuinely stuck processes; startup probes prevent slow boot from being killed too early.

Set resource requests and limits from evidence, handle SIGTERM with graceful shutdown, and keep durable state outside disposable replicas. Deployment success means the service is ready under load, observable and safe to roll back.

title: Angular — State, Rendering and Performance

sections.heading: Measure the expensive boundary
sections.content:
Signals make local dependencies explicit; RxJS remains useful for asynchronous streams, cancellation and external events. Neither fixes a slow API or a huge DOM. Profile the user interaction, use stable list tracking, keep derived template state cheap and paginate or virtualize large collections.

Use component boundaries and OnPush deliberately. Effects are for side effects, not for deriving state that belongs in a computed signal.

title: Architecture — Quality Attributes Drive Trade-offs

sections.heading: There is no universally best architecture
sections.content:
Start with measurable quality attributes: latency, availability, security, operability, changeability and cost. A decision that improves one can harm another; synchronous calls simplify consistency but increase coupling, while events improve decoupling but add delivery and observability concerns.

Record important decisions with context, alternatives, consequences and a review trigger. Architecture is validated by production evidence, not diagrams alone.

title: System Design — Scale the Bottleneck, Not the Diagram

sections.heading: Capacity follows a workload model
sections.content:
Clarify read/write rate, peak patterns, payload size, consistency needs, latency targets and failure behavior before proposing components. Use queues, caches, partitions and replicas only for a named bottleneck.

Every asynchronous boundary needs idempotency, retry policy, dead-letter handling and observability. Define SLOs and load-test the critical path; otherwise a plausible diagram is not a design.

title: SOLID — Keep Change Local

sections.heading: Principles are diagnostics, not ceremonies
sections.content:
Use SOLID to spot coupling and incohesion. Dependency inversion means business code owns the capability it needs while infrastructure implements it; it does not require an interface for every class.

A good abstraction is small, stable and expressed in business terms. If it leaks vendor types, has unsupported methods or changes whenever an implementation changes, the boundary is probably wrong.

title: Design Patterns — Name the Trade-off

sections.heading: Patterns are not architecture by default
sections.content:
A Strategy is useful when an algorithm varies independently and selection remains understandable. Factory centralizes construction when creation policy matters; Observer decouples notifications but needs delivery semantics; Builder improves readable construction for complex valid objects.

Start from the changing behavior and choose the smallest pattern that makes it local. More classes and indirection are costs, not automatic quality.

title: Java Collections — Equality, Mutability and Cost

sections.heading: Choose by semantics first
sections.content:
Use List for ordered duplicates, Set for uniqueness and Map for key lookup. Correctness depends on stable equals and hashCode: mutating a key after insertion can make it unreachable.

Know the trade-off: ArrayList favors indexed reads and append, HashMap gives average constant lookup, and concurrent collections solve particular access patterns rather than making arbitrary compound operations atomic.

title: Java Foundations — Values, References and API Contracts

sections.heading: Reason about state precisely
sections.content:
Java passes arguments by value; for objects, the copied value is the reference. A method can mutate the referenced object but cannot reassign the caller’s variable. This distinction prevents many API and debugging mistakes.

Design methods around clear ownership: validate at boundaries, return immutable views when callers should not mutate state, and document nullability and exception behavior as part of the contract.

title: OOP — Invariants, Collaboration and Change

sections.heading: Objects protect meaningful state
sections.content:
Encapsulation is more than private fields: operations such as approve, cancel and dispatch protect valid transitions. Compose independent policies and collaborators when behavior changes separately; reserve inheritance for stable semantic subtypes.

Good object design makes illegal states difficult to represent, keeps dependencies explicit and gives each rule a clear owner.

title: Kotlin — Nullability as a Boundary Design Tool

sections.heading: Make absence explicit
sections.content:
Kotlin’s non-null default is most valuable when nullability is decided at system boundaries. Convert nullable database, HTTP and Java-platform values into explicit domain choices early; keep internal invariants non-null where possible.

Avoid using !! as an escape hatch. For complex outcomes, model alternatives with sealed results instead of making every caller infer what null means.

title: Behavioral Stories — Evidence, Ownership and Reflection

sections.heading: A strong story has a decision
sections.content:
Use situation, task, action and result, but spend most time on the reasoning: what was uncertain, what options existed, what you personally did and how you measured the outcome. Include a trade-off or lesson learned.

Avoid vague team claims. Be precise about scope and honest about what you would change next time; that demonstrates ownership better than a perfect story.

title: Professional Stories — Preserve Context and Impact

sections.heading: Explain why the work mattered
sections.content:
A technical story is memorable when it connects implementation to people and operations. State the original manual risk, the rule complexity, the system boundary and the changed workflow. Then distinguish observed impact from estimated impact.

Pride belongs in the story when it is grounded in a real transformation: making a difficult, error-prone process dependable is an engineering achievement, not empty self-promotion.

title: Engineering Mindset — Decisions Under Uncertainty

sections.heading: Name the trade-off explicitly
sections.content:
Strong engineers clarify constraints before proposing technology: user impact, data correctness, latency, cost, team skills and operational load. State what is optimized, what is sacrificed and how the decision will be validated.

A reversible small experiment is often better than an unmeasured large redesign. Use production signals and feedback to revisit decisions as assumptions change.

title: Portfolio — Show Evidence, Not a Technology Inventory

sections.heading: Make the engineering story navigable
sections.content:
Lead with the problem, users and workflow; then show the architectural decision, a difficult constraint, quality evidence and a source link. A long stack list has little value without explaining why each choice exists.

Be accurate about personal-project versus production experience. The goal is to demonstrate judgment, delivery discipline and learning depth through verifiable artifacts.

title: Role Research — Connect Evidence Without Overclaiming

sections.heading: Translate, do not imitate
sections.content:
Connect prior work to the target domain through transferable problems: workflow state, integrations, data quality, observability and collaboration. Separate confirmed company facts from reasonable inferences, and do not imply experience with an internal system you have not used.

The strongest connection is concrete: explain one prior decision, the constraint behind it and how you would investigate a similar constraint in the new context.

title: Transactions — Consistency, Proxies and External Effects

sections.heading: Keep the atomic boundary honest
sections.content: A database transaction can atomically protect local changes; it cannot atomically include an HTTP call, email or Kafka publish without a deliberate pattern. Keep the transaction short, understand propagation and isolation, and publish external work through an outbox or after-commit mechanism when reliability matters.

title: JPA Relationships — Ownership, Fetching and Lifecycle

sections.heading: Model ownership before annotations
sections.content: Choose relationships from domain ownership and lifecycle, then make the owning side explicit. Cascades are lifecycle rules, not convenience switches; orphan removal can delete data. Fetch only what a use case needs and avoid collection fetch joins with pagination without measuring duplicated rows.

title: SQL Transactions — Isolation and Lost Updates

sections.heading: Concurrency is part of correctness
sections.content: Isolation determines what concurrent transactions can observe, not whether code is generally safe. Use optimistic versions or conditional updates for competing edits; use stronger locks only when contention and invariants justify them. Design retries for serialization failures and make commands idempotent.

title: API Evolution — Compatibility Before Version Numbers

sections.heading: Evolve contracts deliberately
sections.content: Adding an optional field is often compatible, but strict clients can still break. Removing fields, changing meaning or tightening accepted input needs a migration plan. Use consumer contracts, telemetry, deprecation guidance and an observed migration window before removal.

title: Integration Tests — Fidelity Without Flakiness

sections.heading: Use the real boundary when it is the risk
sections.content: Use the production database engine for dialect, migrations and constraints; stub external HTTP failures deterministically; reserve live smoke tests for controlled environments. Containers improve fidelity but do not create isolation automatically — fixtures, cleanup, readiness and parallel safety still need design.

title: Java Memory Model — Visibility Before Cleverness

sections.heading: Threads need a happens-before relationship
sections.content: Without synchronization, volatile, safe publication or another defined mechanism, one thread may not observe another’s write. Immutable objects are easier to share only when construction completes before publication. Atomicity, visibility and ordering are separate properties; prove each one needed by the operation.

title: Kafka Consumers — Rebalancing, Retries and Idempotency

sections.heading: Consumption is a recovery process
sections.content: Offsets are progress markers, not proof that a business side effect happened exactly once. Design handlers to tolerate duplicates, classify retryable failures, bound retries and route poison messages for investigation. Rebalances mean processing can move; keep consumer work bounded and make state recovery explicit.

title: RxJS — Lifecycle, Cancellation and Data Flow

sections.heading: Streams need ownership
sections.content: Use operators to model asynchronous composition and cancellation, not nested subscriptions. Bind UI subscriptions to component lifecycle, handle loading/error/empty states, and use switchMap when newer input should cancel stale requests. Keep side effects at clear boundaries and expose state the template can render predictably.

title: Compose — Local Environments That Behave Predictably

sections.heading: Start order is not readiness
sections.content: Service DNS and health checks make local dependencies reproducible, but an application still needs retry and timeout behavior because dependencies can fail after startup. Pin versions, expose only needed ports, keep secrets out of committed files and document destructive volume-reset commands.

title: Kubernetes Services — Traffic, Probes and Rollouts

sections.heading: Traffic follows readiness
sections.content: A Service gives stable discovery while Pods change. Readiness must represent ability to serve real traffic; graceful shutdown should first become unready, then finish in-flight work before termination. Test rolling updates under realistic traffic and ensure metrics distinguish startup, dependency failure and overload.

title: Spring Observability — Signals for Operators

sections.heading: Expose decisions, not only JVM numbers
sections.content: Health checks, metrics and traces should answer whether users are succeeding and which dependency is responsible when they are not. Protect management endpoints, keep high-cardinality labels out of metrics, propagate correlation IDs and alert on service objectives rather than every transient technical symptom.

title: Web Security — Browser Boundaries and Trust

sections.heading: The browser is an attacker-controlled client
sections.content: Escape untrusted output to prevent XSS, define CORS from actual origins rather than wildcards, and understand when cookie authentication requires CSRF protection. Content Security Policy, secure cookie flags and server-side authorization complement each other; no client-side check is an access-control boundary.

title: Event-Driven Design — Contracts, Ordering and Recovery

sections.heading: Asynchrony moves complexity
sections.content: Events decouple producers and consumers but introduce delayed visibility, duplicates, ordering scope and recovery paths. Include event identity and version, make handlers idempotent, define retry/dead-letter policy and expose lag and failure signals. Use events where independent consumers and temporal decoupling are valuable, not as a default replacement for every call.

title: Architecture Documentation — Decisions That Stay Useful

sections.heading: Document the why at the right level
sections.content: Keep diagrams focused on a question: context, containers, components or deployment. Pair them with lightweight ADRs that record context, alternatives, decision, consequences and review trigger. Documentation becomes trustworthy when it changes with delivery and links to executable evidence such as tests, dashboards and deployment definitions.

title: Kotlin Coroutines — Structured Concurrency and Cancellation

sections.heading: A coroutine needs an owner
sections.content: Launch work in a scope tied to a request, screen or service lifecycle; avoid GlobalScope for application work. Suspension is cooperative, so use cancellable APIs, propagate cancellation and keep blocking calls on an appropriate dispatcher. Structured concurrency makes failure and cleanup visible instead of leaving background work orphaned.

title: Exceptions — Contracts, Context and Recovery

sections.heading: Exceptions communicate failed contracts
sections.content: Use exceptions for exceptional failure paths with useful context; do not catch broadly just to continue in an unknown state. Separate validation, business conflict and infrastructure failure so callers can decide whether to correct, retry or report. Preserve the cause, add correlation context and avoid exposing internals at API boundaries.

title: Encapsulation — Make Invalid State Hard to Create

sections.heading: Expose intentions, not setters
sections.content: Methods such as reserve, approve and cancel communicate permitted transitions and can protect related invariants atomically. Private fields alone are insufficient when callers receive mutable collections or generic setters. Choose a narrow public API and keep representation changes behind it.

title: SRP — Cohesion Is About Reasons to Change

sections.heading: Split by change pressure
sections.content: A class may have several methods and still be cohesive when they serve one business responsibility. Split when pricing, notification, persistence or formatting change for different reasons and have different owners. Avoid turning every method into a class; unnecessary fragmentation makes workflows harder to follow.

title: Factories — Centralize Creation Policy

sections.heading: Construction can be business logic
sections.content: Use a factory when valid construction chooses implementations, validates combinations or hides infrastructure setup. A factory is not needed merely to replace a constructor. Keep its input expressive and return a stable abstraction only when callers should not depend on the concrete type.

title: JPA Queries — Read Models and Pagination

sections.heading: Query for the use case
sections.content: Read endpoints often need a projection rather than a managed aggregate. Select only required columns, apply deterministic ordering and use keyset pagination when large mutable datasets make offset expensive. Keep authorization filters in every query path and inspect generated SQL for joins and indexes.

title: HTTP Semantics — Retries and State Changes

sections.heading: Idempotent is not harmless
sections.content: GET is safe; PUT and DELETE are idempotent in intended state effect; POST can represent creation or a command. A retry still needs concurrency control, and idempotency does not mean identical responses or no logging. Match method semantics to the resource contract before choosing a URL shape.

title: Conflict — Disagree Productively

sections.heading: Challenge the decision, not the person
sections.content: State the shared goal, bring evidence and clarify constraints before proposing an alternative. Listen for information you do not have, make the decision owner explicit and commit once a decision is made. Retrospect on the result so disagreement improves the next decision rather than becoming personal friction.

title: SQL Joins — Cardinality Before Aggregation

sections.heading: Count rows before trusting totals
sections.content: A join reflects relationship cardinality. Joining two one-to-many child tables can multiply rows and inflate sums; aggregate each child set first or query separately. Use inner joins when absence excludes a row and left joins when absence is meaningful, then inspect actual result sets rather than relying on table names.

title: Kafka Delivery — Exactly Once Is a Scoped Claim

sections.heading: Define the boundary of the guarantee
sections.content: Broker transactions can provide strong guarantees within Kafka, but an external database or HTTP side effect still needs idempotency or an outbox. Name the delivery semantics for each boundary, store a stable event ID and make replay a supported operational path rather than an emergency surprise.

title: Unit Tests — Behavior Over Implementation

sections.heading: Make failures diagnostic
sections.content: A test should set up a meaningful condition, perform one behavior and assert an observable outcome. Mock real boundaries, not value objects or every internal call. Verify interactions only when sending a command is part of the contract; otherwise prefer assertions about state, output or error.

title: Container Builds — Cache and Supply Chain

sections.heading: Fast is not the only goal
sections.content: Order stable dependency inputs before frequently changing source, use multi-stage builds and keep credentials out of layers with secret mounts. Pin base images by digest and lock dependencies for reproducibility. Scan and refresh deliberately: a pinned image is auditable, not permanently safe.

title: Kubernetes Resources — Requests Are Scheduling Contracts

sections.heading: Set limits from measurement
sections.content: Requests affect scheduling; limits constrain runtime behavior. Too-low CPU limits can throttle latency-sensitive services, while memory limits can cause OOM kills. Measure representative load, set alerts for saturation and size autoscaling from demand and downstream capacity, not CPU alone.

title: Angular Forms — Validation as User Feedback and Boundary Defense

sections.heading: Client validation is guidance
sections.content: Use typed form models, explicit validation messages and disabled/pending states to make workflows understandable. Server validation remains authoritative because clients can be bypassed. Map field errors back to controls, preserve user input after recoverable failures and avoid duplicating complex business rules inconsistently.

title: Learning From Mistakes — Improve the System

sections.heading: Turn an error into a control
sections.content: Describe the context, impact, decision and what changed afterward. The useful outcome is not blame; it is a durable improvement such as a test, checklist, alert, review practice or clearer ownership. Be honest about uncertainty while showing how you prevent recurrence.
